subject: Physics
solution:
Resistance of a material is related to temperature by expression R=R01+αΔT

Here, at 10°C, we have  2=R01+10α   ...i

And at 30°C, we have 3=R01+30α    ...ii

From equation i, R0=21+10α 

Putting this value in equation ii, 

3=21+10α1+30α⇒3+30α=2+60α

⇒1=30α

Hence, temperature coefficient of the material of wire is α=130=0.033°C-1

subject: Physics
solution:
Linear expansion of solid with temperature is given by Lf=L01+α∆T

Here, lB1+αBΔT-li1+αiΔT=lB-li

⇒αBlB=liαi

⇒1.8×10-5×40=li×1.2×10-5

⇒li=1.8×10-5×401.2×10-5=3×402=60

⇒li=60 cm

subject: Physics
solution:
Given:

∆Q=184×103 J,

m=0.600 kg at -12 °C,

S=2222.3 J kg–1 °C–1 and

L=336×103J kg-1

Heat required to convert ice from –12 °C to ice at 0 °C will be, 

Q1=0.600×2222.3×12=16000.56 J

Remaining heat Q=184000-16000.56

= 167999.44 J

Heat required to melt ice completely at 0 °C,

Q2 = 0.600 × 336000 = 201600 J needed which is more than Q, so 100% ice is not melted.

For amount of melted ice, we can write

167999.44=m×336000⇒m= 0.4999 kg

∴ mass of water =0.4999 kg

Mass of ice remaining = 0.1001

∴ Ratio =0.10010.4999≈1:5

subject: Physics
solution:
Change in resistance due to temperature is given by, R'=R1+α∆T

For Series Combination:

Req=R1+R2

⇒2R1+αeq∆T=R1+α1∆T+R1+α2∆T

⇒2R1+αeq∆T=2R+α1+α2R∆T

⇒αeq=α1+α22

For Parallel Combination:

1Req=1R1+1R2

⇒1R21+αeq∆T=1R1+α1∆T+1R1+α2∆T

⇒21+αeq∆T=11+α1∆T+11+α2∆T

⇒21+αeq∆T=1+α2∆T+1+α1∆T1+α1∆T1+α2∆T

⇒21+α1∆T1+α2∆T=2+α1+α2∆T1+αeq∆T

⇒21+α1∆T+α2∆T+α1α2∆T=2+2αeq∆T+α1+α2∆T+αeqα1+α2∆T2

Neglecting small terms

2+2α1+α2∆T=2+2αeq∆T+α1+α2∆T

⇒α1+α2∆T=2αeq∆T

⇒αeq=α1+α22

solution:
Suppose m grams of water evaporate. Then, heat required
ΔQreq=mLv
Mass that converts into ice =150-m
Heat released in the process,
ΔQrel=150-mLf
Now, ΔQrel=ΔQreq
150-mLf=mLv
mLf+Lv=150 Lf
m=150LfLf+Lv
m=20 g

0.2mv2=m×125×327-127+2.5×104

v2=5×125×200+2.5×104

subject: Physics
solution:
Given here, mass flow rate of water, dmdt=2 kg min-1

The geyser heats the water, raising the temperature from θ1=30°C to θ2=70°C.

Total heat utilised in raising the temperature, ΔQΔt=dmdt×sΔθ

ΔQΔt=260×4.2×40=mt×L

L= heat of combination=8×103 J g-1

Now, rate of consumption of fuel is mt=2×4.2×4060×8×103

=4.26×10-3=0.7×10-3 kg sec-1

=42 g min-1

subject: Physics
solution: If temperature increases by $\Delta \mathrm{T}$, Increase in length $\mathrm{L}, \Delta \mathrm{L}=\mathrm{L} \alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}$ $\therefore \quad \frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{L}}=\alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}$ Let tension developed in the ring is $\mathrm{T}$. $\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{S}}=\mathrm{Y} \frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{Y} \alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}$ $\therefore \quad \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{S} \mathrm{Y} \alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}$ From FBD of one part of the wheel, $\mathrm{F}=2 \mathrm{T}$ Where, $F$ is the force that one part of the wheel applies on the other part. $\therefore \mathrm{F}=2 \mathrm{SY} \alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Thermal stress in the wire is caused by contraction.
At -43°C, the temperature drop from 27°C is $\Delta T_1 = 70°C$
It creates tension $T = YA\alpha\Delta T_1$ where $Y$ is Young's modulus and $\alpha$ is linear expansion coefficient.
For tension to increase to $1.4T$, the new temperature drop must be: $\Delta T_2 = 1.4 \times 70 = 98°C$.
Required temperature: $T_3 = 27 - 98 = -71°C$

$$

subject: Physics

Let S be the specific heat of aluminium

Q given = Q used

⇒1000×0.2×S×(150−40)=150×1×(40−27)+25×1×(40−27)

⇒200×S×110=150×13+25×13

⇒S=13×175200×110=0.1034 cal g-1 °C

⇒S=103.4 ×4.2 J kg-1 °C=434 J kg-1°C

subject: Physics
solution:
According to Newtons law of cooling.
$\frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}} \propto-\left(\theta-\theta_0\right)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\mathrm{d} \theta}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\mathrm{k}\left(\theta-\theta_0\right)$
$\int \frac{d \theta}{\theta-\theta_0}=\int-k d t$
$\Rightarrow \quad \ln \left(\theta-\theta_0\right)=-k t+c$
Hence the plot of $\ln \left(\theta-\theta_0\right)$ vs $t$ should be a straight line with negative slope.

$\begin{aligned}
& \Delta T=T_2-T_1=16^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \\
& \text { And } T_0=16^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \\
& \frac{\Delta T}{t_1}=-k\left(32-16^{\circ}\right)...(i) \\
& \frac{\left(24-T_3\right)}{4}=-k\left(\frac{24+T_3}{2}-16\right) ....(ii) \\
& \frac{16}{4}=-k(16) \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{\left(24-T_3\right)}{4}=-k\left(12+\frac{T_3}{2}-16\right) \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{16}{24-T_3}=\frac{16}{T_3} T_2 \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{T_3}{2}-4=24-T_3 \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{3 T_3}{2}=28 \\
& \Rightarrow T_3=\frac{56}{3}{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}
\end{aligned}$

Let diameter be d0 at 27 °C and d' at 177 °C. Therefore, we can write

d'=d01+αΔT

Therefore, change in diameter will be

⇒d'-d0=5×1.6×10-5×177-27

=12×10-3 cm

Hence, required value of d=12.

solution:
Given specific heat of the water 4200 J kg-1 °C-1

Latent heat of the water =2260  kJ kg-1  

Q=P×t

Q=mcΔT+mL

⇒P×t=mcΔT+mL

⇒Vrms2R×t=mcΔT+mL

⇒200220×t=4200×80+2260×103

⇒t=1298 s≃22 min
